query: US labor market conditions April 2026 tariffs impact employment layoffs
answer:
Now I have extensive data. Let me compile the final research report.

---

# US Labor Market Conditions — April/May 2026: Tariff Impact on Employment & Layoffs

## Executive Summary

The US labor market in early 2026 exhibits a paradoxical "low-hire, low-fire" equilibrium. While initial jobless claims hit a 56-year low (189,000 in the week ending April 25) and the unemployment rate moderated to 4.3% in March, hiring has slowed to pandemic-era lows and tariff-exposed industries—particularly manufacturing and transportation—have hemorrhaged jobs. The economy lost 189,600 blue-collar jobs in the 10 months following "Liberation Day" (April 2, 2025). The labor market remains under pressure from overlapping shocks: tariffs, the Iran war (begun February 28, 2026), federal workforce reductions (DOGE), and AI-driven restructuring.

---

## 1. Latest Employment Situation Data

### March 2026 (released April 3, 2026)
- **Nonfarm payrolls: +178,000** — the largest gain in 15 months, far above consensus of ~60,000
- **Unemployment rate: 4.3%**, down from 4.4% in February
- **Average hourly earnings: +0.4% m/m to $37.32**

### February 2026 (released March 6, 2026)
- **Nonfarm payrolls: -92,000** — an unexpected loss; consensus was +60,000
- **Unemployment rate: 4.4%**
- January payrolls revised down from +130,000 to +126,000

### January 2026 (released February 11, 2026)
- **Nonfarm payrolls: +130,000** (later revised to +126,000)
- **Unemployment rate: 4.3%**
- 2025 benchmark revisions revealed employers added **fewer than 200,000 jobs in all of 2025** (vs. ~1.5 million in 2024)

### April 2026 Report
- Scheduled for release **May 2, 2026** — data not yet confirmed in my sources.

---

## 2. Initial Jobless Claims

### Week Ending April 25, 2026
- **Initial claims: 189,000** — a plunge of 26,000, the **lowest since September 1969**
- **4-week moving average: 207,500**
- **Continuing claims: 1.79 million** (week ending April 18)

### Recent Weekly Trajectory
| Week Ending | Initial Claims |
|-------------|---------------|
| Apr 25, 2026 | 189,000 |
| Apr 11, 2026 | 207,000 |
| Mar 22, 2026 | 210,000 |
| Mar 15, 2026 | 205,000 |

HFE Chief Economist Carl Weinberg cautioned: *"There is nothing to worry about in this report. YET! At some point, elevated energy costs and prices for materials will cause firms to lay off marginal workers to protect profit margins."*

---

## 3. JOLTS Data — Job Openings & Hiring

### February 2026 (released March 31, 2026)
- **Job openings: 6.9 million** — a larger-than-expected decline
- **Hiring fell to the lowest level since the pandemic**
- Layoffs edged down

### December 2025 (released February 5, 2026)
- **Job openings dropped to the lowest level since 2020**
- Layoffs edged up

**Key dynamic:** The "low-hire, low-fire" paradox — employers aren't laying off workers, but they're barely hiring either. Those who lose their jobs face historically long search times.

---

## 4. Tariff Impact on Employment by Sector

### Manufacturing
- **89,000 manufacturing jobs lost** in the 10 months following "Liberation Day" (April 2, 2025 – February 2026)
- Equivalent to approximately **2,800 average-size factory closures** (Center for American Progress)
- Manufacturing activity contracted for **8 consecutive months** post-Liberation Day

### Transportation & Warehousing
- **123,700 jobs lost** in the same 10-month period
- Challenger data: **703% year-over-year increase** in Q1 2026 transportation job cuts — highest Q1 on record

### Total Blue-Collar Jobs
- **189,600 blue-collar jobs lost since Liberation Day**
- In **45 of 50 states**, blue-collar job creation under tariffs fell short of Biden-era annual averages
- Average state lost 2,500+ blue-collar jobs vs. average annual gain of 7,400 previously

---

## 5. Announced Layoffs (Challenger Data)

### Q1 2026 Totals: 217,362 job cuts announced
- Down 56% from Q1 2025 (497,052, which included massive federal workforce cuts)
- Down 16% from Q4 2025 (259,948)

### Q1 2026 by Industry
| Industry | Q1 2026 Cuts | YoY Change |
|----------|-------------|------------|
| Technology | 52,050 | +40% |
| Transportation | 32,241 | +703% (record) |
| Healthcare | 23,520 | Record Q1 |
| Education | 11,467 | +170% |
| Financial | 9,397 | -41% |

### March 2026 specifically
- **60,620 job cuts** — up 25% from February's 48,307
- **AI was the leading cited reason** for job cuts in March

### Notable Company Actions (2026)
- **Whirlpool:** 341 layoffs at Amana, Iowa facility (Feb 2026), despite being a vocal tariff supporter — moved production to China
- **Harley-Davidson:** Worldwide layoffs began March 2026; profits fell 26% as "tariffs erase profits"
- **Amazon:** ~16,000 corporate job cuts (Jan 2026)
- **Nike:** 775 employees cut, accelerated automation (Jan 2026)
- **Morgan Stanley, Block, UPS, Dell, Oracle, Meta:** All announced cuts in early 2026

---

## 6. Federal Reserve Assessment

### April 28-29, 2026 FOMC Decision (most recent)
- **Rate held at 3.50%-3.75%**
- **4 dissents** — highest since October 1992 (33+ years)
- Statement cited "solid economic activity, low job gains, and elevated uncertainty including from the Middle East conflict and energy prices"
- Likely **Powell's final meeting as Chair** (term ends May 2026)

### Key Fed Forecasts (March 2026 SEP)
- Unemployment: **4.4% by year-end 2026**
- Core PCE inflation: **2.7%** (raised from 2.5%)
- GDP growth: **2.4%**

### Market Expectations
- Fed funds futures: **No cuts priced through at least July 2026**
- J.P. Morgan: Predicts **zero cuts in all of 2026**

---

## 7. Consumer Confidence & Broader Context

### University of Michigan Consumer Sentiment
| Month | Index | Notes |
|-------|-------|-------|
| Jan 2026 | 56.4 | Well below neutral (80) |
| Feb 2026 | 56.6 | |
| Mar 2026 | 55.5 | Lowest of 2026 |

### Broader Macro Context
- **Q1 2026 GDP: 2.0%** (advance estimate, April 30)
- **CPI inflation: 3.3% y/y** (March 2026)
- **PCE inflation: 3.5% y/y** (March 2026)
- **Core PCE: 3.2%** (March 2026)
- **Gas prices: ~$4.30/gallon** (national average, late April)
- **Oil: ~$104/barrel**
- **US tariff rates ended 2025 above 15%** (up from ~2.5% at start of 2025), with limited deescalation expected
- **Goldman Sachs recession probability: 30%** over next 12 months
- **Small-business bankruptcies up 10%** over past year; large corporate bankruptcies at highest since 2010

---

US unemployment rate was 4.3% in March 2026, down from 4.4% in February
February 2026 nonfarm payrolls fell by -92,000, a shocking miss against consensus of +60,000
Initial jobless claims fell to 189,000 for week ending April 25, 2026 — the lowest level since September 1969
Continuing claims stood at 1.79 million for week ending April 18, 2026
JOLTS job openings fell to 6.9 million in February 2026; hiring reached the lowest level since the pandemic
189,600 blue-collar jobs lost in the 10 months following Liberation Day (April 2, 2025), including 89,000 manufacturing and 123,700 transportation/warehousing jobs
Challenger reported 217,362 job cuts in Q1 2026; transportation cuts surged 703% YoY to record levels
AI was the leading cited reason for job cuts in March 2026 (60,620 cuts announced, up 25% from February)
The Federal Reserve held rates at 3.50%-3.75% at the April 28-29 FOMC meeting with 4 dissents — the highest dissent level since 1992
The Fed's March 2026 SEP projected year-end unemployment at 4.4% and core PCE inflation at 2.7%
US employers added fewer than 200,000 jobs in all of 2025 per benchmark revisions, down from ~1.5 million in 2024
University of Michigan consumer sentiment fell to 55.5 in March 2026, the lowest reading of the year
Q1 2026 GDP grew at 2.0% (advance estimate), below expectations
PCE inflation reached 3.5% year-over-year in March 2026, the biggest increase in nearly 3 years
Gas prices averaged ~$4.30/gallon nationally by late April 2026; oil at ~$104/barrel
Goldman Sachs raised US recession probability to 30% over the next 12 months
Whirlpool laid off 341 workers while moving production to China; Harley-Davidson began worldwide layoffs as tariffs erased profits
In 45 of 50 states, blue-collar job creation under tariffs fell short of Biden-era annual averages
J.P. Morgan predicts zero Fed rate cuts through all of 2026; fed funds futures price no change through at least July
